Fnrn Polrrrcel Pnncrrcps CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95g14_2-329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
January 7,2009
D. Thacker
2029 Moss Valley Drive
Fairfield, CA94534
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-09-229
Dear Mr. Thacker:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act;).1 Beiause your question, * 6ured on a general outline
lf ? Tu"t glan for campaign fundraising, we are treating your request as one for informal
asslstance'' Please bear in mind that we are not authorii"a to give advice on bodies of Iaw
outside the bounds of the Act, and we are therefore unable to offer advice on state law governing
the circumstances under which oral contracts may or may not be legally uinJing, or on state or
federal law on loan terms, interest rates, usury, oi other regulatory Jor."*, thai may or may not
be pertinent to your planned activities.
QTIESTIONS
l' May a candidate solicit donations or loans to his or her campaign by offering to repay any
donations or loans to the donor with 100 percent interest, should the candidate win the
election?. No. return of the contributio, oi lo* would be offered should the candidate
tose tne electlon.
2. How would these contributions be disclosed?
3' May the terrns be stated in an oral contract, or must any agreement be in writing?
Irh" PolititulReform Act is contained in Government code Secrions g1000 rhrough gl0l4. All staturory
references are to the Government code' unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the california coae of negulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2' Division o of ttri california Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section g3l 14; Regulaiion 1g329(c) i3).) 
rrv ' rsvu ur o. r


File No. I-09-229
Page No. 2
(l') No' Contributions from the candidate himself, repaid with interest substantially in
excess of the time-value of the funds, w.ould amount to a prohibited use of campaign funds to
provide compensation to the candidate for his activities as candidate. Contributions from third-
party contributors, also repaid with interest well above the time-value of the funds, would be a
prohibited use of campaign funds to make gifts to the conrributors. In light oi it i, conclusion to
Question 1, Questions 2 and 3 are not addrissed.
CONCLUSION
T.ACTS
ANALYSIS
^ 
You expect to run for Solano county Assessor in the June 2010 election, and have
formulated a plan to. assist your campaign fundraising trrrougtr a novel incentive program. ln
telephone conversations with the commission's Tecinical Assistance Division on November 4
andNovember23,2009, andinasubsequentemailmessage, youaskedif youmaypay 100
percent interest on contributions to youriampaign, whethe"r tt.y *, -ua"Ly yourself or by
other persons' You indicated that these repayments would be contingeot on you, success in the
election, and that the repayment agreements would be verbal, but milht be reducea to writing if a
written agreement is required by law. Finally, you asked how the incoming and outgoing
payments would be reported, if your contingeni interest-bearing repaym."t"pr." is otherwise
permissible.
We do not know how long your campaign's obligations might remain unpaid, and thus
the "annual percentag" t1t:" earned by your contributori tr ooubli the amount were repaid
within six months, the APR would be 2ilo percent or more. If repaid in two yl*r, the ApR
would be reduced to 50 percent. The annual rate of return on any given donation depends on the
order in which you repay the contributors and, more generally, onlou, post-e1"ction fundraising
success' information presumably not ascertainable at-this point. we therefore discuss these
transactions without considering their duration, but urru*. for purpose, of tt i, *alysis that you
anticipate relatively short-term obligations promising a contingent return of twice the amount of
each contribution.
section 852Olrequires' among other things, that on filing a Statement of Intention to run
for office, a candidate must set up a "i-paign contribution account at an office of a financial
institution located within the state, that ail contributions or loans made to assist the candidate in
the campaign must be deposited into this account, and that all campaign.^plrrai,ures must be
made from this campaign account. Further, all funds deposited into th-is account are subject to a
trust described by Sicti,on 89510(b):
"(b) All contributions deposited into the campaign account shall be deemed to be
held in trust for expenses associated with the eleition of the candidate or for
expenses associated with holding office.,'


File No. l-09-229
Page No. 3
The Act recognizes that loans are a form of contribution, which may be used to fund
election campaigns whether the loans are made by the candidate to his or hlr own campaign, or
by third parties'i However, the Act also imposes"'personal use" restrictions that bar candidates,
elected officials, and other committee personnel from using campaign funds to confer financial
benefit on themselves or others. (Sections 89510 - sgs22;the genleral rule is that an
expenditure of campaign funds must be reasonably related to a political, Iegislative or
govemmental purpose. But when campaign funds are used to ctnfer u ruult*tiul personal
benefit on any individual with authority to lggrwe an expenditure of .urpuit, funds, rhe
expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative, or govemmental purpose.
(Sections 895 10(b); 89512.5.)
Section 89518(a) more narrowly provides that campaign funds shall not be used to
compensate a candidate for the performance of political, legislative, o, gor"**ental activities,
except for reimbursement of certain out-of-pocket expenses. candidates for elective state office
are prohibited from obtaining interest on loans made io the campaign from their own personal
resources under Section 85307(b), but the Act does not prevent caniidates for other offices from
receiving interest on their loans to the campaign. However, there is a distinction between an
interest rate that recognizes the time-value or ;opportunity costs" of money loaned for a period
of time to a campaign, and an interest ra'te that faiexceeds the cost of rnutirg trre loan, yielding a
substantial net gain for the lender. we conclude that a 100 percent interest rate, which would
double a candidate's money in what may be a quite short period of time, u-oun,, to
"compensation" to the candidate, ratherih* u ,n.., return of interest p"..i*iui" under the Act.
Therefore section 895r8(a) bars you from_making a personar loan t" y"r.;;;aign at an
interest rate that substantially overvalues the reut -"ori or any loan you make to the committee.
You have also asked about contributions from third-party contributors who would also be
promised a 100 percent retum, if you are elected.4 Persons oth"rt'ir" unrelated to you and
unconnected to your campaign are not subject to "personal use" restrictions, properly so called,
since payments to these persons do not result in personal benefit to the "*aiaut" or campaign
staff' There is, however, an important restriction on the use of campaign funds that is germane to
your proposal. Section 89513(f)(l) provides:
"Campaign funds shall not be used to make personal gifts unless the gift is
directly related to..a political, legislative, or gou"*m"rrtal purpose. rie refund of
a campaign contribution does not constitute th" making of u g-ift.,,
3 Note, however' that Section 85307(b) imposes a $ 100,000 limit on personal loans a candidate for elective
state office may make to his or her campaign, and piohibits the candidate from charging interest on loans he or she
has made to the campaign.
a The term 'tontribution" of course includes a loan to a campaign committee which, if properly disclosed,
may permit a reasonable return on the principal. we do not have information on the precise terms you have in mind,
but your proposed 100 percent return on what seems to be a short-term transaction, ai noted in the first paragraph of
this Analysis' seems far in excess of a "reasonabre" rate of return.


File No. l-09-229
Page No.4
Here also we must distinguish between the return of a contribution and the retum of a
sum twice as large which, as we understand your proposal, cannot be construed as simply
reflecting the time-value of the contribution ieporit"a in your campaign account. your implicit
purpose is to offer a financial incentive to encourage contributionsio !o*.u*puign - the
incentive being an opportunity of making a twofold return on the -orgy within a relatively short
space of time, subject to the risk that yo, *ay not be elected;;ffi.".i'w;;;"*" that potential
contributors would assess this risk in the weeks before the election and, as you evidently
anticipate, would be influenced at least in part to make a contribution to your campaign by their
anticipation of substantial personal gain.
We conclude that a refund of a campaign contribution is not a prohibited ..gift,, under
section 89513(0(l), but a payment twice the rulu" of a contribution is a windfall retum
amounting to a "personal gift" within the meaning of this statute. we must then ask whether
payment of a 100 percent interest premium to a cJntributor is a gift directly related to a political,
legislative or govemmental purpoie. We cannot find that such an arrangement is directly related
to any purpose expressly authorized by the Act.
vitually all califomia election campaigns at all levels are, and long have been, funded
by contributions to the candidates without any promise of direct monetary ;",u-. occasionally
candidates or their supporters will extend loans to a campaign, but we are not aware of any such
transactions whose l"^"": provide for repayment at an inlerest rate far above ..market rates,,
customarily charged for loans essentially rl*ita, in all material respects. Against this
background of established "trade practi;e," a proposal to return contributio^ i., tt " 
event of your
success' and to double the money "loaned" by your contributors, makes it impossible for us to
conclude that this expenditure of campaign funds is "directly related" to the political, legislative
or governmental purpose required Section g9513(0(l)
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
LnWU
LTwjgl
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
5 As noted above, our advice is confined to the provisions of the Act; we do not offer any opinion on the
Iegality of your plan under other state or federal laws.
